title: Array Division
description:
You are given an array containing n positive integers.

Your task is to divide the array into k subarrays so that the maximum sum in a subarray is as small as possible.

Input:
- First line: two integers n and k — the size of the array and the number of subarrays in the division.
- Second line: n integers x_1, x_2, ..., x_n — the array contents.

Output:
Print one integer: the minimum possible value of the largest subarray sum among all valid divisions into k subarrays.

The array must be divided into contiguous subarrays, preserving order.

examples.explanation: One optimal division is [2,4], [7], [3,5] with maximum subarray sum 8. No division into 3 subarrays achieves a smaller maximum.

mcqChain.questionText: What does “divide the array into k subarrays” mean in this problem?

options.label: Split into k contiguous parts preserving the original order.
Pick any k disjoint subsets regardless of positions.
Split into k parts after sorting the array.
Group elements into k parts where order can be rearranged within parts.

mcqChain.explanation: Subarrays are contiguous segments of the original order. Reordering or picking arbitrary subsets is not allowed.

wrongExplanations.1: Subsets ignore contiguity; that is not allowed here.
wrongExplanations.2: Sorting changes order and is not permitted.
wrongExplanations.3: Order within each part must remain as in the array.

mcqChain.hint: Subarrays are continuous stretches of the original array.

mcqChain.questionText: If k = 1 for array [5, 1, 4], what must the answer be?
mcqChain.explanation: With one subarray, it contains all elements, so the maximum subarray sum equals the total sum 5+1+4=10.

wrongExplanations.1: 5 is the maximum element, not the sum of the single subarray.
wrongExplanations.3: 6 is not related to any valid single-subarray measure here.

mcqChain.hint: One subarray must include every element.

mcqChain.questionText: If k equals the number of elements n for array [5, 1, 4], what is the smallest possible maximum subarray sum?

options.label: 3 (rounded average)

mcqChain.explanation: Each element can be its own subarray; the largest subarray sum is the maximum element, 5.

wrongExplanations.1: 10 is the total sum; we can do better by splitting into singletons.
wrongExplanations.2: Average is irrelevant; we need the max subarray sum, not average.

mcqChain.hint: Each subarray can hold exactly one element when k=n.

mcqChain.questionText: For array [2,4,7,3,5] and k=3, which division achieves the smallest possible maximum subarray sum?
mcqChain.explanation: The best here is [2,4], [7], [3,5] with maximum 8. Others have maximum 10 or 13.

wrongExplanations.2: [4,7] sums to 11, making max at least 11 (plus [3,5] is 8).

mcqChain.hint: Compute the sum of each subarray and compare the largest per division.

mcqChain.questionText: Are we allowed to reorder elements before dividing into k subarrays?

options.label: No; the order must be preserved and subarrays are contiguous.
Yes; we can reorder to balance sums better.
Yes; but only within each chosen subarray.
We can sort equal elements only.

mcqChain.explanation: Order must be preserved and subarrays are contiguous by definition.

wrongExplanations.1: Reordering changes the problem and is not allowed.
wrongExplanations.2: Reordering within subarrays breaks contiguity of original array.

mcqChain.hint: Stick to the original order.

mcqChain.questionText: Why is trying all ways to place k-1 cuts infeasible for n up to 2⋅10^5?

options.label: The number of cut choices grows combinatorially (like choosing k−1 positions out of n−1).
Because we need to sort before cutting.
Because sums cannot be computed fast enough even once.
Because memory will always run out first.

mcqChain.explanation: There are about C(n−1, k−1) partitions; this explodes for large n. We need a more directed search.

wrongExplanations.1: Sorting isn’t required for correctness, and its cost is not the main blocker.
wrongExplanations.2: We can compute sums linearly; the issue is the number of partitions.

mcqChain.hint: How many ways to choose k−1 cut points among n−1 gaps?

mcqChain.questionText: Given a candidate limit M for the largest subarray sum, how can we check if the array can be split into at most k subarrays so each has sum ≤ M?
mcqChain.diagram: Scan left to right while maintaining a running sum.

options.label: Scan once: keep a running sum; when adding next would exceed M, start a new subarray.
Pick exactly ⌊n/k⌋ elements per subarray regardless of values.
Sort the array then take chunks that sum to ≤ M.
Try all cut positions but stop early if too many subarrays are needed.

mcqChain.explanation: A single pass works: accumulate, and start a new subarray when needed. Sorting or fixed-size chunks break contiguity or correctness.

wrongExplanations.1: Chunking by count ignores values and may violate ≤ M.

mcqChain.hint: Think: when does the current subarray end as you add elements?

mcqChain.questionText: As M increases, how does the possibility of splitting into at most k subarrays with each sum ≤ M change?

options.label: Once possible at some M, it remains possible for all larger M.
Once possible at some M, larger M can make it impossible again.
Possibility toggles unpredictably with M.
It is only possible for exactly one specific value of M.

mcqChain.explanation: If you can fit within M, allowing more capacity (larger M) cannot hurt. So the feasible set is an upper interval.

wrongExplanations.1: Larger limits relax constraints; they won’t invalidate feasibility.
wrongExplanations.2: There can be many feasible values, not a single one.

mcqChain.hint: Compare M and M+1 for the same partitioning method.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the smallest possible value the answer can take (a lower bound)?

options.label: The maximum element of the array.
The minimum element of the array.
The average of all elements divided by k.

mcqChain.explanation: Each subarray must contain its elements; none can be split. So the maximum subarray sum is at least the largest single element.

wrongExplanations.1: 0 is impossible since all elements are positive.
wrongExplanations.3: Average doesn’t account for the largest element constraint.

mcqChain.hint: No subarray can have sum less than the largest element it contains.

mcqChain.questionText: What is a valid initial upper bound for the answer?

options.label: The sum of all elements.
The sum divided by k.
k times the maximum element.
The minimum element.

mcqChain.explanation: Putting all elements into one subarray gives an upper bound equal to the total sum.

wrongExplanations.1: Sum/k can be too small to fit the largest element.
wrongExplanations.2: k·max could exceed the true answer and is not a tight guarantee.

mcqChain.hint: Consider the one-subarray division.

mcqChain.questionText: Using the scan method on [2,4,7,3,5] with M=8, how many subarrays are needed?
mcqChain.diagram: Accumulate until exceeding 8, then start a new subarray.
mcqChain.explanation: We get [2,4], [7], [3,5] → 3 subarrays, all sums ≤ 8.

wrongExplanations.1: 2 is too few; [2,4,7] already exceeds 8.

mcqChain.hint: Track the running sum and reset when adding next exceeds 8.

mcqChain.questionText: Using the same array with M=7, how many subarrays are needed by the scan method?
mcqChain.explanation: [2,4], [7], [3], [5] → 4 subarrays due to the 7 and sum constraints.

wrongExplanations.1: 3 is not enough because [3,5] would exceed 7.

mcqChain.hint: The pair [3,5] cannot stay together under 7.

mcqChain.questionText: During the check with a limit M, what should happen if any element x > M?

options.label: Immediately conclude it is impossible with this M.
Continue and try to split that element across two subarrays.
Increase k by 1 and continue.
Ignore that element for the check.

mcqChain.explanation: An element cannot be split; if x > M, no subarray can hold it, so this M fails.

wrongExplanations.1: Elements cannot be divided across subarrays.
wrongExplanations.3: k is fixed; we cannot change it during checking.

mcqChain.hint: Subarrays must contain whole elements.

mcqChain.questionText: You maintain a range [low, high] for the answer. If a candidate M in this range is feasible (works), which side should you explore next to find the minimum?

options.label: Try smaller values (move high down).
Try larger values (move low up).
Pick randomly within the range.
Stop immediately; any feasible M is minimal.

mcqChain.explanation: We seek the smallest feasible M, so we should try the lower side when current M works.

wrongExplanations.1: Larger values won’t help find the minimum.
wrongExplanations.3: A feasible M might not be minimal.

mcqChain.hint: We want the smallest M that still works.

mcqChain.questionText: Which loop condition and boundary updates will correctly converge to the minimal feasible M?

options.label: While low < high: mid=(low+high)//2; if feasible: high=mid; else: low=mid+1.
While low <= high: mid=(low+high)//2; if feasible: low=mid+1; else: high=mid-1.
While low < high: mid=(low+high)//2; if feasible: low=mid; else: high=mid.
While low <= high: mid=(low+high)//2; if feasible: return mid; else: try another mid.

mcqChain.explanation: This scheme narrows to the minimal feasible M and terminates when low==high.

wrongExplanations.1: This update searches for the largest feasible M, not the smallest.
wrongExplanations.2: These updates can get stuck and don’t ensure convergence.

mcqChain.hint: When feasible, keep the current mid as a possible answer and try smaller.

mcqChain.questionText: Define the checker function header and initialize its counters correctly.
mcqChain.explanation: Start with one subarray (count=1) and current sum 0. The function needs the limit parameter.

wrongExplanations.1: Starting at 0 subarrays miscounts the first group.
wrongExplanations.2: Missing the limit parameter prevents checking sums against it.

mcqChain.hint: We begin before processing any elements with one subarray and 0 sum.

mcqChain.questionText: Add the loop over elements and the early impossibility check.
mcqChain.explanation: If any element exceeds limit, no split works. Don’t reject when current+next exceeds; that triggers a new subarray, not failure.

wrongExplanations.1: Exceeding with current sum should start a new subarray, not fail.
wrongExplanations.2: Equal to limit is allowed; only strictly greater should fail.

mcqChain.hint: Only a single element bigger than limit makes it impossible.

mcqChain.questionText: Fill the logic to either extend the current subarray or start a new one.
mcqChain.explanation: If within limit, add x; otherwise start a new subarray (increment count) and set current to x.

wrongExplanations.1: Using < causes an extra split when equal to limit; current=0 loses x.
wrongExplanations.2: Swapping updates breaks accumulation and splitting behavior.

mcqChain.hint: On split, the new subarray starts with x; on no split, add x.

mcqChain.questionText: Return whether the number of subarrays used fits within k.

options.label: return count <= k
return count < k
return count == k
return True

mcqChain.explanation: We allow at most k subarrays, so count must be ≤ k.

wrongExplanations.1: Strictly less would reject valid cases using exactly k subarrays.
wrongExplanations.2: Requiring exactly k may reject feasible cases with fewer subarrays.

mcqChain.hint: At most k, not exactly k.

mcqChain.questionText: Read input n, k and the array from two lines as described.

options.label:
n, k = map(int, input().split())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))

n = int(input())
k = int(input())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))

n, k = map(int, input().split())
arr = [int(input()) for _ in range(n)]

n, k = input().split()
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))

mcqChain.explanation: The problem provides n and k on one line, and all array values on the next line.

wrongExplanations.1: This expects k on a separate line; the input may not be formatted that way.
wrongExplanations.2: This expects n subsequent lines; the values are on a single line.

mcqChain.hint: Read two lines: first for n and k, second for the array.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
n, k = map(int, input().split())
arr = list(map(int, input().split()))

mcqChain.questionText: Initialize bounds and set up the narrowing loop and mid computation.

options.label:
low = max(arr)
high = sum(arr)
while low < high:
    mid = (low + high) // 2

low = 0
high = sum(arr)
while low < high:
    mid = (low + high) // 2

low = max(arr)
high = sum(arr)
while low <= high:
    mid = (low + high) // 2

low = max(arr)
high = sum(arr)
while low < high:
    mid = (low + high + 1) // 2

mcqChain.explanation: Lower bound is max element; upper bound is total sum. Using while low < high with mid=(low+high)//2 works with the chosen updates.

wrongExplanations.1: Starting at 0 may try limits below the largest element, which cannot work.
wrongExplanations.2: Using <= requires different updates to ensure termination.

mcqChain.hint: The lowest possible limit can’t be below the largest element.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
low = max(arr)
high = sum(arr)
while low < high:
    mid = (low + high) // 2

mcqChain.questionText: Fill in the decision step to shrink the search range based on feasibility of mid.
mcqChain.explanation: With while low < high, keep mid when feasible by setting high=mid; otherwise push low up to mid+1.

wrongExplanations.1: Using high=mid-1 with this loop can skip the minimal feasible and cause off-by-one issues.
wrongExplanations.3: Setting low=mid can stall if mid equals low when feasible.

mcqChain.hint: When feasible, include mid; when not, exclude mid.

mcqChain.questionText: Finally, print the result after the loop terminates.

options.label: print(low)
print(high)
print(mid)
print(ans)

mcqChain.explanation: low equals high at termination and is the minimal feasible limit. We did not maintain an ans variable.

wrongExplanations.1: high equals low here, but printing low is consistent and clear.
wrongExplanations.3: There is no ans variable in this approach.

mcqChain.hint: At the end, low==high is the answer.

mcqChain.questionText: Trace the first iteration for [2,4,7,3,5], k=3: low=7, high=21 ⇒ mid=14. The check with M=14 is feasible. What happens to the bounds?
mcqChain.diagram: low=7, high=21, mid=14; with M=14, we need at most 3 subarrays.

options.label: high becomes 14, low stays 7
low becomes 15, high stays 21
low becomes 14, high stays 21
high becomes 13, low stays 7

mcqChain.explanation: Feasible ⇒ set high=mid to try smaller maximums next.

wrongExplanations.1: Increasing low would try larger limits, contrary to minimizing.
wrongExplanations.2: Setting high to mid−1 is not what our loop uses.

mcqChain.hint: Feasible → shrink the upper bound to mid.

mcqChain.questionText: Running the full algorithm on [2,4,7,3,5] with k=3, what value is printed?
mcqChain.explanation: As reasoned earlier, the minimal maximum subarray sum is 8 via [2,4], [7], [3,5].

wrongExplanations.1: 7 fails because [3,5] exceeds 7.
wrongExplanations.2: 9 and 10 are feasible but not minimal.

mcqChain.hint: We already checked M=8 is feasible and M=7 is not.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time to check feasibility for a fixed M on an array of length n?

options.label: Proportional to n (single pass).
Proportional to log n.
Proportional to n log n.
Constant time.

mcqChain.explanation: We scan once, doing O(1) work per element, so it’s proportional to n.

wrongExplanations.1: We touch each element; it’s not logarithmic.
wrongExplanations.2: No sorting or extra logs per element are done.

mcqChain.hint: How many passes over the array does the check need?

mcqChain.questionText: How many times will we test different M values before converging (roughly)?
mcqChain.diagram: Range is from max(arr) to sum(arr); we take midpoints repeatedly.

options.label: About the number of halving steps of the range (≈ 60 for 64-bit sums).
About n times.
About the square root of the range.
About the size of the range itself.

mcqChain.explanation: Each step halves the search interval; for large sums this is around a few dozen iterations.

wrongExplanations.1: We don’t try all elements’ worth of distinct M.
wrongExplanations.3: We don’t linearly scan all possible M.

mcqChain.hint: Each step cuts the interval roughly in half.

mcqChain.questionText: Which edge case must the checker handle explicitly to avoid incorrect success?

options.label: If any element exceeds the candidate limit, declare failure immediately.
If k is greater than n, increase n.
If there are zeros, skip them entirely.
If the array is not sorted, sort it first.

mcqChain.explanation: A single element > limit cannot fit; this must be caught. Other cases either can’t happen by constraints or are irrelevant.

wrongExplanations.1: k ≤ n by constraints; we don’t change input.
wrongExplanations.3: Sorting is not allowed and unnecessary.

mcqChain.hint: Think about an element larger than the allowed subarray sum.

reasoningChain.question: What does dividing into k subarrays mean here?
reasoningChain.insight: Subarrays must be contiguous and preserve order.

reasoningChain.question: What are tight lower and upper bounds for the answer?
reasoningChain.insight: Lower bound is max element; upper bound is total sum.

reasoningChain.question: How to check if a candidate maximum M is achievable with ≤ k subarrays?
reasoningChain.insight: Scan once, accumulating and starting a new subarray when adding would exceed M; fail if any element > M.

reasoningChain.question: How does feasibility change as M increases?
reasoningChain.insight: Feasibility is monotonic: once feasible, all larger M are feasible.

reasoningChain.question: How to home in on the minimal feasible M?
reasoningChain.insight: Maintain [low, high]; test mid; if feasible, move high to mid; else move low to mid+1; stop when low==high.

reasoningChain.question: How to implement and verify correctness on examples?
reasoningChain.insight: Assemble the checker and narrowing loop, then trace on sample to ensure the answer matches expected.
